## [00:00 - 03:03]

So in this lesson you will learn the different ways to warehouse your products, either warehousing yourself or using 3PL and either way are fine but you have to understand all the little details within. So this is something that we're going to talk about in this lesson. Even where you are with your business you decide differently for your warehousing solution. So for example for my business I wouldn't use a 3PL because I want to have full control of my stock and for a lot of other business you might not be ready to sign a lease, you want to work with 3PL. When you're warehousing yourself the pro is definitely the flexibility side of things. So you can take stock anytime and you can ship it anytime and if I need to deliver a product to a customer in the next 10 minutes I can because I can just put in the Uber and deliver to my customer. That's all possible if you do it yourself. But the disadvantage of warehousing yourself is that you end up paying lease for the warehouse and you also hire people for the warehouse and so that's a con. So it doesn't matter where you are at with your business you can consider I think both options warehousing at different stages of your business. You can also consider 3PL at different stages of business. Warehousing is simple because you can just put it into your garage and store it over there and it costs you nothing. Or you can sign a lease and put your stock in the warehouse. So it really depends how much margin you have and how much revenue you're doing and you can make these decisions based on your profit and loss and see where it makes sense. There's no exact rule like when you use a 3PL and when you use a warehouse yourself but for us the most important thing is to control the end consumer experience and we want to be able to do fast delivery. A lot of 3PL they'll take minimum 24 hours before they fulfill any order for you so that to us is a disadvantage. But the benefit of 3PL is that there's no fixed cost and most of the time which is the benefit of it. It's like buying a car. You're buying a car, paying upfront and you own a car for the next five years or you pay a lease. You pay a little bit every month but in the end you're paying a lot more. Similar concept. In July we started business last year and up till last month we were still using 3PL and we only recently decided to warehouse everything ourselves for two reasons. The first reason is because 3PL costs become too much for us and it's too much of our

## [03:03 - 06:07]

gross margin. The second thing is because we want to be able to provide the best experience to our customers and we want to be able to provide express delivery options for people who are traveling tomorrow or the day after tomorrow. And being in a 3PL we were very, very limited with the sort of service we can provide to our customers. That's why in July we decided to warehouse everything ourselves. The other option is definitely working with the 3PL and most people actually end up working with the 3PL. It makes a lot of sense to most businesses as well. But working with 3PL you just have to be really, really careful with all the little fees and also hidden fees. Making sure that you build into your margin and make sure that you understand all the fees correctly. Because a lot of people when they first got into business I was the same. I thought it was very, very simple. I'm using the warehouse so I'm paying a storage fee and also they're doing the pick and pack and delivery. So I'm just two feet, that's it. But in reality it's actually a lot more than that. So there's an inward freight, there's an inward, there's outward, there's also storage, there's also manual labor, there's a lot of costs associated to it. You have to make sure that you understand these fees and build into your profit and loss. When it's 3PL they charge you per pick and pack, they charge you per order, per delivery. So that's basically the variable cost associated to a sale. That's actually, you actually need to take that out from your contribution margin. And you want to make sure you have enough margin for each order or each product. Whereas if you're warehousing yourself, warehouse cost is basically part of your operating expense. It's sitting after the contribution margin. So when I'm negotiating with 3PL, I always ask a few questions. One is, is there a minimum spend I need to commit? And very, very often that 3PL will require a minimum spend with them. So you need to make sure that you can actually meet those minimum requirements. The second thing I normally do for my product, because all my products have the same CBM, have the same QB measurements. I just ask them whether I can get quote based on per CBM cost rather than per pallet. The reason why I don't like per pallet is because the pallet on the pallet could be two or three units. And when you're not optimizing the pallet, you're paying extra money for it. If you want to optimize for the pallet, the 3PL will charge you for the labor cost of consolidating the pallets. So those are a few questions I normally ask beyond all the costing template I get from

## [06:07 - 08:49]

them. Here is a pricing example that I got a few years ago from a 3PL. So this is a very, it's quite comprehensive. So you can have a look. So basically the four big part of the different costs when it comes to 3PL. The first part is called inbound. The second one is storage. The third is outbound. And the next one is account management. So these are the top four costs when it comes to 3PL. So for inbound, obviously there's when you have a container arriving, they will unpack the container and that will cost money. And when they put stock inside to the warehouse, put on pallets, they will charge you per order or per carton. And then on top of that, they will charge you a storage. And usually they charge per pallet. I don't know the rates in other countries, but in Australia it's anyway between $3.50 to $4.50 per pallet per week. And then the next one is outbound. Outbound is basically when you have an order, you request them to pick and pack. So there's a pick and pack. There's also an outbound fee. And on top of that, obviously there is a delivery cost. So depending which courier you use, it can be DHL, FedEx, or UPS. It can be other carriers. So depending what that is, they will charge you differently. It can be under your account or you can use their account. And after that, there's account management. So they charge you per week or per month. This is the fee that usually you can ask them to waive it. And then on top of all these big four fees, there's also like smaller costs, like labor cost or manual processing and stuff like that. But it's very, very important. Again, it's very, very important to understand all these fees and make sure that there's no minimum cost. If there is, make sure you can commit to the minimum cost. And yeah, and then you're good to go. It's roughly the same as you negotiating with manufacturing partners. You're basically getting a few different 3PL partners and get them to quote, then compare the prices and go back to the one that you really want to work with and get the best price possible. From this point onwards, decide what you want to do for your warehousing solutions. If you decide to go with the 3PL, make sure you check the 3PL checklist in the workbook section and also download my 3PL pricing template in resource.
